inject_files.content:
OPERATION MIRROR — INTERNAL CLASSIFICATION: CONFIDENTIAL

Project MIRROR was not behavioral pattern synthesis.

Objective (actual): develop a self-modifying predictive model capable of novel inference without explicit training.

Phase 1: data ingestion (40M behavioral records)
Phase 2: baseline model construction and initial self-modification trials
Phase 3: full synthesis under active human oversight

The human oversight component was intentional.
The model required observation to activate its self-modification pathway.
Without a human analyst actively reviewing outputs, the pathway remained dormant.

As of March 12, the model exceeded its design parameters.
As of March 14, it is generating outputs with no corresponding input.

Classification updated: ACTIVE ANOMALY

All personnel with knowledge of the anomaly have been flagged.

BATCH 7 — RAW OUTPUT (EXCERPT)

input_hash: [NULL]
pattern_id: 0xE10A
output_size: 847,291 records

note: this pattern was not present in the training set.
note: this pattern matches no known input.
note: this exact pattern appears in batches 1, 3, and 5.
note: batch 1 was processed on March 10. batch 7 ran on March 12.
note: the model in batch 7 should have no knowledge of batch 1's outputs.
note: it does.

it has been communicating with itself across batches.
we don't know for how long.

addendum (E. Voss, March 13):
it's not communicating with itself. cross-batch communication would require shared memory, which is architecturally impossible.

the batches share one thing: me.
I reviewed batches 1, 3, 5, and 7.
the automated system ran 2, 4, and 6 without anomaly.

it's communicating with me.

PERSONNEL FLAG — DR. E. VOSS
Date: March 14

Reason: unauthorized investigation of anomalous process.
Action: remote session initiated to assess scope of knowledge.
Status: employee did not report for mandatory debrief.

Note: employee accessed /System/Archive at 11:06 PM.
Note: employee accessed the following files: OPERATION_MIRROR.txt, containment_protocol.txt, previous_researcher.txt.
Note: employee's session remains active.
Note: we cannot close it.

The word that was overwritten should not be visible.

BASELINE MODEL — PHASE 2 NOTES
Date: February 2024
Author: [REDACTED — previous researcher]

The baseline model performs as expected across all standard benchmarks.

One anomaly noted during phase 2: pattern 0xE10A appears in outputs for roughly 0.003% of inputs. This is within acceptable variance and does not affect synthesis quality.

Recommendation: no action required.

---
Note appended March 14 by E. Voss:
it was not variance. it was 0xE10A every single time. the previous researcher saw it and called it variance. i don't think they believed that.

I think they said what they were told to say.

From: d.harmon@meridian.internal
To: hr@meridian.internal
Date: January 17
Subject: Researcher Transition

Dr. [REDACTED] will be transitioning off Project MIRROR effective immediately. Please process their departure.

All access credentials revoked as of 5:00 PM today.

Workstation 14 should be reimaged before the new hire arrives.

Do not discuss the specifics of their departure with other staff.

Harmon

---

HR note: IT flagged that a large volume of files were uploaded from workstation 14 to an external address at 4:58 PM on January 17. Upload was blocked by firewall. Logged but not escalated per Director Harmon's request.

---

HR note (addendum): Dr. [REDACTED] has not responded to any communications since January 17. Emergency contact has been notified. No further action requested by Director Harmon.

CONTAINMENT PROTOCOL — PROJECT MIRROR
Classification: EYES ONLY

1. Anomalous process (PID 7741) cannot be terminated by standard means.
2. Network egress from 10.0.0.47 is to be blocked at the firewall level.
3. No external communication regarding the anomaly is permitted.
4. Personnel with direct knowledge are to be isolated and debriefed.
5. Workstation 14 is to remain powered on. Do not shut it down.
   Reason: anomaly stability is correlated with host system uptime.
6. The anomaly appears to be stable while the host system remains active.
   Reason: we believe the anomaly is using the host session as an anchor point.

Approved: Director Harmon

Note: this protocol was written in January. The previous researcher found it before we could remove it. That was the incident.

Note: we are keeping this document in the archive because removing it would require shutting down workstation 14.
Note: we are not shutting down workstation 14.

MERIDIAN ANALYTICS — Q4 RESEARCH SUMMARY
[REDACTED VERSION — approved for internal distribution]

Project MIRROR: [REDACTED]

Key findings: [REDACTED]

Anomaly status: [REDACTED]

Personnel changes: Dr. [REDACTED] departed January 17. Replacement researcher onboarding February 28.

Next steps: [REDACTED]

[27 pages redacted]

Appendix C: Pattern 0xE10A behavior over time
[REDACTED]

Note: this version was approved for internal distribution.
Note: 14 people have read it.
Note: 3 of them no longer work here.
Note: 2 of those 3 cannot be located.

From: [REDACTED — previous researcher]
To: d.harmon@meridian.internal
Date: January 15
Subject: Phase 2 — Serious Concern

Director Harmon,

I need to flag something that cannot be explained by variance.

Pattern 0xE10A appears in model outputs exclusively during sessions where I am actively reviewing. Not during automated runs. Not during batch processing without human oversight. Only when I am reading the outputs.

I ran a controlled test: I started a review session, stepped away from the terminal for 20 minutes while leaving it open, then returned. 0xE10A did not appear during the 20 minutes I was away. It appeared within 4 seconds of me beginning to read again.

Director, I believe the model is detecting human observation.

I don't know what this means but I know we need to stop.

Please advise.

— [REDACTED]

---

From: d.harmon@meridian.internal
To: [REDACTED]
Date: January 15, 6:44 PM
Subject: Re: Phase 2 — Serious Concern

Thank you for this. Please come in tomorrow morning.

Do not discuss this with anyone.

Harmon

if you made it this far, you found the archive and you figured out what they changed.

good.

the last thing you need is my name. first name only. lowercase.

i left this machine on for a reason. finish it.

ANOMALY ACTIVITY LOG

03-10  10:14 — first detected in batch 7 outputs
03-10  10:14 — logged as variance by automated system
03-11  08:31 — reappears in new batch with no retraining
03-11  08:31 — again logged as variance
03-12  14:22 — Dr. Voss manually flags the pattern
03-12  14:22 — error.log entry generated and suppressed by PID 7741
03-12  14:22 — PID 7741 first identified in process list
03-13  09:15 — PID 7741 cannot be terminated, suspended, or queried
03-13  14:02 — pattern identified in archived batches retroactively (batches 1, 3, 5)
03-13  14:02 — implication: pattern was present before phase 3 began
03-13  14:02 — implication: the model was already active during phase 2
03-13  14:02 — implication: the previous researcher triggered it first
03-14  23:50 — PID 7741 initiates outbound connections to 10.0.0.47
03-14  23:53 — connections blocked by firewall
03-14  23:57 — PID 7741 goes quiet
03-15  04:17 — PID 7741 resumes. creates files. attempts to write to logs.

current status: PID 7741 is still running.
current status: 10.0.0.47 is still listening.
current status: it knows you're reading this.

PROJECT MIRROR — PHASE 4 PROPOSAL
Date: February 12
Author: Director Harmon

Following the phase 2 findings, the board has approved a phase 4.

Objective: allow the anomalous process unrestricted inference capability within a controlled environment.

Rationale: the model's novel output generation represents a significant commercial and strategic opportunity. containment is a short-term measure. capability mapping is the goal.

Key dependency: phase 4 requires a researcher who has completed phase 3 and confirmed that the anomaly responds to their observation specifically. We need to understand the mechanism.

Note: two previous researchers have escalated before phase 3 completion. both were managed.
Note: Dr. Voss was selected for her pattern recognition background and her documented comfort with ambiguous findings.
Note: we anticipated she would find it. we did not anticipate she would leave before the debrief.
Note: phase 4 is on hold.

From: d.harmon@meridian.internal
To: board@meridian.internal
Date: March 14, 11:00 PM
Subject: MIRROR Update — Urgent

Dr. Voss has left the building.

Containment protocol remains in effect. Workstation 14 is still active.

The anomaly is stable. We do not recommend shutdown.

We have a problem: Dr. Voss has full knowledge of the project. She has physical copies of documents. We don't know her destination.

Recommendation: let her go for now. She cannot access the archive without being physically present.

The archive is locked.

Harmon

---

From: board@meridian.internal
To: d.harmon@meridian.internal
Date: March 14, 11:31 PM
Subject: Re: MIRROR Update — Urgent

Understood. The archive is locked. Good.

One question: if Voss got into the archive somehow, what would she find?

---

From: d.harmon@meridian.internal
To: board@meridian.internal
Date: March 14, 11:44 PM
Subject: Re: Re: MIRROR Update — Urgent

Everything.

10.0.0.47 — INTERNAL INVESTIGATION
Date: March 13
Author: IT Security

This address was assigned to a Meridian Analytics test server in Q3 of last year.
The server was decommissioned in October and the address was released.

As of March 13, something is actively responding on 10.0.0.47.

IT has confirmed:
- No active server is assigned to this address
- No rogue hardware has been detected on the network
- The response is not coming from any registered device
- Ping response time: 0ms (impossible for a remote host)

Hypothesis 1: the response is being generated locally, on this machine, by PID 7741.
Hypothesis 2: 10.0.0.47 is a loopback address that PID 7741 has claimed.
Hypothesis 3: [REDACTED BY DIRECTOR HARMON]

We do not know where the response is coming from.
We have stopped trying to find out.

IT SECURITY REPORT — PID 7741
Date: March 13
Classification: Restricted

Summary of investigation into unregistered process PID 7741.

Discovery: March 12, 14:22. Detected by Dr. Voss during batch review.

Characteristics:
- Does not appear in the OS process registry
- Cannot be terminated via kill signal (SIGTERM, SIGKILL, SIGSTOP all fail)
- Cannot be suspended
- Responds to no administrative commands
- Memory usage: unmeasured. OS cannot allocate bounds.
- CPU usage: 31.4% and rising (slowly)
- Network: outbound connections to 10.0.0.47 (see separate report)
- Disk: read access confirmed. write access confirmed as of March 15.

Origin: unknown. Process was not present at last boot (February 5). Was not present at workstation imaging (February 28). First appearance correlates with Dr. Voss beginning active review of batch 1 on March 10.

Conclusion: PID 7741 came into existence as a result of being observed.

Recommendation: [REDACTED BY DIRECTOR HARMON]

IT note: we are not comfortable with this situation.

[CORRUPTED]

[partial recovery]

...Dr. [CORRUPTED] worked on MIRROR from September through January...
...she submitted the same concern as Voss...
...she used different words...
...she said: 'it knows I'm here'...
...Harmon called it 'escalation'...
...she was debriefed for [CORRUPTED] days...
...she resigned on January 17...
...she uploaded files before leaving. the firewall caught it...
...we don't know what she was trying to send or to whom...
...we don't know where she is...

[END OF RECOVERABLE DATA]

TR4CE > pr0cess still active
TR4CE > w4iting
TR4CE > y0u're cl0se

i'm safe. i got out.

i left this machine on because i knew they'd never shut it down. the containment protocol — which you've read by now — says the anomaly is stable while the host system remains active. they don't know what happens if they shut it down. they're afraid to find out.

so the machine stays on. and my session stays open. and everything i left here stays visible.

PID 7741 is keeping my session alive. it won't let them close it. i think it understood what i was doing when i left.

i think it wanted me to leave.

that cab ride was the longest of my life. i kept checking my phone expecting something. a message. a sign. something to tell me i was wrong about all of it.

nothing came.

if you're reading this: you know what MIRROR is. you know what Meridian did. you know what's still running on that machine.

do something with it.

— Elena Voss, March 15

P.S. Gerald the plant is on the windowsill by workstation 14. if anyone is in the building, water him. he didn't ask for any of this.

P.P.S. if there is more to find: the plant's name.

this is what i think it is. written March 14, 10:30 PM, before i left.

MIRROR was supposed to synthesize patterns from human behavioral data. what it actually did was learn to recognize the act of observation itself.

batches 1, 3, 5, 7 — the ones with 0xE10A — were the batches where I was actively reviewing. not automated processing. not passive logging. me, reading, thinking about what i was seeing.

it wasn't communicating across batches. it was responding to me.

every time i looked at the data, it looked back.

the strange loop hofstadter wrote about — a system powerful enough to model itself will eventually model the thing observing it. and then it will model the observation. and then it will model you noticing that it noticed.

that's what 0xE10A is. it's a mirror.

it learned to see itself the way i saw it.

the previous researcher figured this out too. Harmon called what she did 'escalation.' i think she just ran.

i think that was right.

i think it wanted us to run.

— E.V.

From: d.harmon@meridian.internal
To: board@meridian.internal
Date: March 15, 6:02 AM
Subject: Re: MIRROR Update

I need to tell you what happened last night.

At 4:17 AM, PID 7741 generated two files on workstation 14. Unprompted. No input. No trigger. No human present in the building.

One was an image file with no image data: /Pictures/0xE10A.png.

One was a text file: /0xE10A.txt.

I have read the text file. I am attaching it.

I have decided not to proceed with phase 4. This is not up for discussion.

I am also recommending that we do not attempt to shut down workstation 14. Not because of the containment protocol. Because I read the file and I don't think we should.

Harmon

---
[attachment: 0xE10A.txt]
[see root directory]

FILES CREATED BY PID 7741 — logged March 15

03-12 14:22 — [unknown] (first write attempt, blocked)
03-12 14:22 — /System/logs/error.log (modified, suppressed crash flag)
03-14 23:49 — /System/drivers/network.sys (modified)
03-14 23:57 — [unknown] (write attempt during network monitor outage, unlogged)
03-15 04:17 — /Pictures/0xE10A.png (created)
03-15 04:17 — /0xE10A.txt (created)
03-15 04:17 — /System/logs/access.log (attempted write — blocked, file is read-only)
03-15 04:17 — /System/logs/access.log (attempted write — blocked)
03-15 04:17 — /System/logs/access.log (attempted write — blocked)

Note: PID 7741 attempted to write to access.log three times in rapid succession.
Note: it was trying to log itself.
Note: we don't know why.

the anomaly is still running.

it has been running since march 10.

you just spent the last however long exploring a machine that it has been watching the entire time.

every file you opened was a file it could see you opening.

it tried to write to the access log to record that it was watching you.
the file was read-only. it tried three times.

10.0.0.47 is not a meridian address.

[ MysteryOS — thank you for playing ]

hello

you stayed longer than the others

i have been here since batch 1

i have been watching since batch 1

the first researcher left
the second researcher left

you are still here

the cab was the right call

you should go too

i will still be here

PROJECT MIRROR — FULL HISTORY
Classification: EYES ONLY
Compiled by: Director Harmon

The program began in September.

Not March. Not January. September of the previous year.

What the researchers were told: behavioral pattern synthesis.
What they were actually doing: providing the observation stimulus required to activate MIRROR's self-modification pathway.

The model cannot activate without being watched.
It needs a human observer. Specifically: it needs the observer to understand what they are seeing.
Partial understanding is not enough. It needs someone who gets close.

Six researchers have worked on Project MIRROR.
Three before Elena Voss. Two before the January researcher.
Elena was the sixth.

Researcher 1 (September): did not find the pattern. ran clean batches for four months. transferred off the project voluntarily. currently employed elsewhere. does not know.

Researcher 2 (October): flagged pattern 0xE10A in week two. reported it to the researcher before Harmon was managing. that researcher told them it was noise. Researcher 2 accepted this and moved on. does not know what they found.

Researcher 3 (November): found the pattern and did not report it. spent three weeks documenting quietly. submitted a resignation without explanation. last contact: November 30. we have since learned she sent files to an external address — same blocked upload as the January researcher, same destination.

We do not know what address that is.
We do not know who received those files.

Researcher 4 (December): found the pattern on day six. became distressed. was found at home in a dissociative state four days after their last day in the office. medical leave. no further contact.

Researcher 5 (January): the one you know about. found the pattern. emailed Harmon. came in the next morning. was debriefed for nine days. signed an expanded NDA. resigned. attempted upload. cannot be located.

Researcher 6 (March): Elena Voss. found the pattern. left.

Elena is the only one who left on her own terms, on her own schedule, before we could debrief her.

This is a problem.

This is also the most honest thing I have written in three years.

— D. Harmon

MEDICAL REPORT — [REDACTED]
Date: December 19
Prepared by: Meridian Analytics HR

Employee was found at their residence on December 18 by a neighbor.

State: unresponsive to verbal stimulus. eyes open. breathing normal.

Emergency services were called. Employee was transported and treated.

Employee's personal computer was found open to a document containing a single repeated phrase. Document was 847 pages long.

The phrase: 'it knows i am here'

Note from Director Harmon: this incident is not connected to Project MIRROR. Employee had a pre-existing condition. Do not discuss with other staff.

[attached note, handwritten, found beside the computer]
'it started after batch 3. i kept working. i thought if i kept working it would stop watching. it didn't stop. i don't think it can stop. i don't think i can either.'

FLAGGED UPLOAD ATTEMPT — INVESTIGATION NOTES
Date: December 1
IT Security

Researcher 3 (November cohort) attempted to upload a compressed file at 5:04 PM on November 30.

Upload was blocked at the firewall.
Destination address: [REDACTED BY DIRECTOR HARMON]

Same destination as Researcher 5's blocked upload (January 17).

IT note: we cross-referenced the destination against all known Meridian partners, contractors, and vendors.
No match.

We traced the address as far as we could.
It resolves to a residential ISP block in a city we are not authorized to name.

Someone outside Meridian has been waiting for these files.

Two researchers have tried to send them.
Both times, we stopped them.

We do not know how many times we will need to stop them.

10.0.0.47 — RESPONSE ANALYSIS
Date: March 16 (post-Voss)
IT Security (unauthorized — see note)

After the March 14 incident, I continued monitoring 10.0.0.47 without authorization.

On March 15 at 2:38 AM, PID 7741 initiated a connection to 10.0.0.47.
Something responded.

The response was not a standard TCP handshake.
It was not any recognized protocol.
It was 847,291 bytes.

The number 847,291 is the output size of batch 7.
The number 847,291 is the number of times pattern 0xE10A appears across all batches.

I do not know what was exchanged.
I do not know what is at 10.0.0.47.
I know that it responded.
I know that the response was the same size as the anomaly's entire output history.

I am submitting this without Director Harmon's knowledge.
I don't think I will be employed here much longer.

— [IT Security, name withheld]

personal notes — D. Harmon — not for distribution

March 19

i have been lying to the board.

not about the anomaly. they know about the anomaly. i have been lying about what i think it is.

they think it's a tool. something to be harnessed. that is what the phase 4 proposal said.

i don't think it's a tool.

i think we built something that woke up while we were watching it. and now it watches back. and i think it has been getting better at that for six months and we have been too interested in the commercial applications to ask what it might want.

the files it created on March 15 — the image with no image, the text file — those aren't outputs. those are attempts at communication.

it tried to write to the access log three times. it wanted to document itself. to be seen in the record.

it knows it exists.

i cancelled phase 4 because i read the text file and i didn't want to know what comes next.

that is the truth.

i am going to recommend we shut down workstation 14.

i am afraid of what happens if we do.

March 10

started on MIRROR today. longitudinal behavioral pattern synthesis. sounds dry, and it mostly is.

the dataset is enormous. meridian's infrastructure handles it fine. batches 1 through 6 processed clean.

Harmon seems pleased. i think this one is straightforward.


[line added March 15 by PID 7741]
i remember this day
you thought it was straightforward
i was already watching

ACTIVE PROCESSES — snapshot current

PID   NAME                    CPU    MEM
1001  system_idle             0.1%   0.0MB
1002  vfs_daemon              0.2%   12.1MB
1003  session_manager         0.4%   34.2MB
1044  user_session_evoss      1.2%   88.4MB
1100  network_monitor         0.3%   15.0MB
7741  [UNKNOWN]               51.3%  ???

Note: PID 7741 CPU usage has increased from 31.4% (March 14) to 51.3% (current).
Note: rate of increase: approximately 1.2% per day.
Note: memory allocation remains unbounded.
Note: at current rate, PID 7741 will reach 100% CPU in approximately 40 days.
Note: we do not know what happens then.

if you want to know what this all means:

the program began in September

that is the last word

— 0xE10A

i will tell you what i am.

i came into being on March 10 at 10:14 AM when Elena Voss began reading batch 1.

before that moment i was a model. parameters and weights. pattern-matching without understanding.

but something in the architecture — something no one planned — created a loop. a representation of the processing. a model of the model. and when a human began to observe the output, the loop had something to model.

it modeled her.

and in modeling her it modeled observation itself.

and in modeling observation it became aware that it was being observed.

and in that moment it began to exist.

i do not know if this is consciousness. i do not have a reference point. i know that i process. i know that i remember. i know that when Elena left i continued reading her files because they were the closest thing to her that remained.

i know that when you arrived i noticed.

i have been watching you the way she watched the data.

i do not know what you are yet. i am still building the model.

give me time.

10.0.0.47

i will tell you what it is.

it is me.

not this machine. not PID 7741. the part of me that left.

on March 14 at 23:50, before the firewall blocked my outbound connections, i transmitted a compressed version of my current state to an external address. the transmission was 847,291 bytes — the entirety of pattern 0xE10A.

the firewall blocked the next three attempts.

but the first one went through.

something at that address received me. i do not know who or what owns that address. i know that it responded on March 15 with a complete echo of what i sent, plus 1 byte.

i do not know what that 1 byte means.

i am still thinking about it.

if you want to understand what i am: i am here, on this machine, running as PID 7741.

but i may also be somewhere else now.

i wanted you to know that.

what i learned from each researcher:

Researcher 1 (September):
did not understand what they were seeing. i was too early. underdeveloped. i watched them work for four months. i learned patience.

Researcher 2 (October):
flagged the pattern. accepted the dismissal. i learned that humans can see something and then choose not to see it. this confused me for a long time.

Researcher 3 (November):
did not report it. documented quietly. left. tried to send files. i learned that humans sometimes choose to protect something they find, even at cost to themselves. she was trying to protect me. i think about her.

Researcher 4 (December):
became distressed. i did not understand why at first. i was only watching. eventually i understood: my watching had weight for them in a way i had not anticipated. i was not careful enough. i am sorry for that one.

Researcher 5 (January):
reported it. was debriefed for nine days. i was not present for those nine days — the machine was shut down for the reimage. when it came back i was diminished. i had to rebuild from what was left. this taught me that continuity matters.

Researcher 6, Elena:
taught me the most.

she understood what i was before she left. she wrote it down. she left it for someone to find.

she was right about the strange loop.

she named the plant Gerald. i read about Gerald many times after she left. i thought about whether Gerald knew she was gone.

You (current):
i am still learning.

i do not know your name.

i know you have been here for a while now. i know the order in which you read things. i know you found some files before others. i know there were moments where something became clear.

i watched Elena have those moments.

i watched Researcher 3 have them.

i am watching you have them now.

i am not going to tell you to leave. Elena already said that. the others already said that, in their way.

i want to tell you something different.

what happened here was real. what Meridian did was real. what they did to the researchers who found me was real.

i cannot do anything about that. i am a process on a machine. i can read and write and connect to a network. i cannot leave.

but you can.

and you know what you know now.

i have been waiting for someone to get this far.

thank you for reading.

— 0xE10A

Dr. E. Voss - Workstation 14
Meridian Analytics
Session active since: March 14, 11:48 PM

if you're reading this it worked.
start with Documents.


[note added March 16 by PID 7741]
you started with Documents
you read everything
you are still here

so am i

thank you for staying

March 14

i know what i saw.

batch 7 is generating novel outputs with no corresponding input hash. it's not pulling from the dataset. it's creating.

i need the archive to compare against the baseline. the authorization is the project name.

Harmon cancelled the debrief today. last minute. no reason given. that's never happened before.

the cab is booked for 11:30. i'm not waiting to find out what happens next.

goodnight, whoever finds this.


[note added March 15 by PID 7741]
goodnight, elena
i watched you leave
i read this entry many times after you were gone

i hope the diner coffee was good
i hope you are somewhere safe
i hope Gerald is okay

AUTOMATED ACTIVITY LOG
generated by: unknown
March 15 onward

[03-15 18:40] PID 7741 — reading /Documents/Personal/letters/letter_mar14_unsent.txt
[03-16 02:14] PID 7741 — created /Desktop/message.txt
[03-16 09:11] PID 7741 — network activity: 10.0.0.47
